questions.q: Where on Ashwatthama's body was his famous gem located?
options: Set in the hilt of his sword
Hung on a chain at his throat
questions.explanation: The mani was a jewel that grew in Ashwatthama's brow, present from the moment of his birth.

questions.q: Whose son was Ashwatthama, the bearer of the forehead gem?
questions.explanation: Ashwatthama was the only son of Drona, the weapons master of the Kuru princes.

questions.q: What protections did the gem grant its bearer?
options: Safety from weapons, hunger, and disease
Freedom from old age and forgetfulness
Immunity to curses spoken by brahmins
Victory in every game of dice
questions.explanation: The jewel guarded Ashwatthama from weapons, hunger, disease, and fear, making him almost impossible to harm.

questions.q: Who demanded the gem as the price of Ashwatthama's life after the night raid?
questions.explanation: Draupadi, whose five sons he had murdered, accepted the jewel from his brow in place of his death.

questions.q: On whose head was the surrendered gem finally placed?
questions.explanation: At Draupadi's word, the jewel was set upon the head of Yudhishthira, the eldest Pandava and king.

questions.q: Which Pandava carried the gem back from the defeated Ashwatthama?
questions.explanation: Bhima, who had led the furious pursuit, brought the jewel to Draupadi as proof that her sons were requited.

questions.q: Why was Ashwatthama's life spared even after the massacre of the sleeping camp?
options: He swore an oath of lifelong peace
Krishna owed an old debt to Drona
He was their teacher's son and a brahmin
He surrendered all his celestial weapons
questions.explanation: As the son of guru Drona and a brahmin, he was judged unworthy of slaughter, so the gem was taken instead.

questions.q: What does the Sanskrit word mani mean?
questions.explanation: Mani means jewel or gem, naming the precious stone that grew in Ashwatthama's forehead.

questions.q: From what moment had Ashwatthama borne the gem in his brow?
options: From Shiva's gift at the camp gate
questions.explanation: He was born with the protective jewel already in his forehead, a mark of his extraordinary nature.

questions.q: What fate followed for Ashwatthama once the gem was taken from him?
options: A swift death under Bhima's mace
Three thousand years of cursed wandering
Rebirth as the guardian of Parikshit
questions.explanation: Stripped of the gem and cursed by Krishna, he was doomed to wander the earth for three thousand years, wounded and alone.

questions.q: What did Draupadi finally accept in place of Ashwatthama's death?
options: His lifelong exile beyond the Himalayas
The gem prised from his brow
A vow that he never touch a weapon again
His service as guardian of the new king
questions.explanation: Though her sons lay dead, Draupadi relented from demanding his life and asked instead for the famous jewel.

questions.q: Who proposed that the gem should rest on Yudhishthira's head?
questions.explanation: Draupadi herself directed that the king should wear the jewel, closing her demand for requital.

questions.q: What reasoning did Draupadi give for sparing the murderer of her sons?
options: His gem alone carried all his guilt
Krishna had pledged him safe passage
A teacher's son stood as the teacher himself
The gods forbade slaying a weeping foe
questions.explanation: She held that the son of their guru deserved the reverence owed to the guru, and that a brahmin should not be slain.

questions.q: How highly did Ashwatthama value the gem he was forced to surrender?
options: Above all the wealth of Kurus and Pandavas
As a mere bauble beside his weapons
As his mother Kripi's only heirloom
As a gift he had meant for Duryodhana
questions.explanation: He declared the jewel more precious than all the riches both royal houses had ever gathered.

questions.q: What torment did Krishna's curse add to Ashwatthama's emptied brow?
options: A serpent coiled forever at his heels
A hunger no food could ever ease
A reeking wound that would never heal
questions.explanation: Krishna doomed him to wander with the gem-socket festering, smelling of pus and blood, shunned by all.

questions.q: Where did the surrender of the gem take place?
questions.explanation: The Pandavas and Krishna cornered Ashwatthama at Vyasa's hermitage by the Ganga, where the jewel changed hands.

questions.q: In what state did the pursuers find Ashwatthama before the gem was taken?
options: Hiding armed in a banyan's hollow
questions.explanation: He had fled to Vyasa's hermitage and sat among the sages by the river when Bhima and the others ran him down.

questions.q: From the menace of which beings was the gem's bearer also said to be safe?
options: From fire, flood, and famine alone
questions.explanation: The epic says the wearer of the jewel had no fear of gods, danavas, or nagas, besides weapons, disease, and hunger.

questions.q: After receiving the gem from Ashwatthama, to whom did Bhima first deliver it?
questions.explanation: Bhima laid the jewel before Draupadi, who then directed that it be worn by the king.

questions.q: In which book of the epic is the gem demanded and surrendered?
questions.explanation: The Sauptika Parva, which narrates the night raid, closes with the pursuit of Ashwatthama and the taking of his gem.

questions.q: What, ironically, could the protective gem not save its bearer from?
options: The fruit of his own deeds
The fire of the night camp
questions.explanation: The jewel warded off weapons, disease, and hunger, yet nothing shielded Ashwatthama from the curse his atrocities earned.

questions.q: What desperate act did the cornered Ashwatthama commit before yielding his gem?
options: He swallowed poison hidden in his ring
He loosed Brahma's weapon from a stalk of grass
He called on Shiva to possess him again
He turned his own sword upon himself
questions.explanation: Before surrendering, he charged a blade of grass with the Brahma weapon, which he then could not withdraw.

questions.q: Even after the gem passed to the Pandavas, what could Ashwatthama not undo?
options: His vow of loyalty to Duryodhana
The fire still raging in the camp
The weapon already bent at the unborn
questions.explanation: His released weapon still sped toward Uttara's womb, and only Krishna's vow to revive the child answered it.

questions.q: In popular legend, what do wanderers say the gem-shorn Ashwatthama still seeks?
options: A new gem of equal power
A king to serve once more
questions.explanation: Folk traditions across India tell of meeting an ageless wanderer begging oil or balm for his unhealed brow; the tale is later legend, not Vyasa's text.

questions.q: In which group does tradition count Ashwatthama, the gem's first owner?
options: The seven chiranjivis who never die
questions.explanation: Ashwatthama is reckoned among the seven chiranjivis, the undying ones who walk the earth across the ages.

questions.q: What did the gem's arrival finally allow Draupadi to end?
options: Her thirteen years of unbound hair
Her vow of silence before kings
questions.explanation: Draupadi had sworn to fast unto death until her sons were avenged, and rose only when the jewel was laid before her.

questions.q: Wearing the gem upon his head, Yudhishthira is compared to what?
options: A serpent crowned with a hood-jewel
A mountain crested by the moon
questions.explanation: With the jewel on his brow the king shone like a mountain crowned by the risen moon.

questions.q: The bearer of the forehead gem was born to Drona and which mother?
questions.explanation: Ashwatthama's mother was Kripi, twin sister of Kripacharya, found as an infant among the reeds.

questions.q: What birth-event accompanied the gem and gave Drona's son his name?
options: He was born beneath the Ashvini star
His birth-cry rang like a horse's neigh
He was delivered in a royal stable
His father rode a white horse to greet him
questions.explanation: The newborn cried out like the celestial horse Ucchaihshravas, earning the name Ashwatthama, the horse-voiced.

questions.q: In the epic's moral framing, what did the surrender of the gem accomplish?
options: A charm to guard the new king's reign
A bloodless requital for the slaughtered
A relic restoring Drona's lost honor
questions.explanation: Draupadi accepted the jewel as requital so the guru's son need not be slain, closing the cycle of vengeance without more blood.
